title: Ephesians 2
subtitle: Made Alive in Christ, United as One Body

sections.header: Verses 1–10 — "By Grace You Have Been Saved through Faith"

heb.paragraph: The noun charis appears twelve times in Ephesians, with the densest concentration in 2:5–8. The programmatic statement ‘by grace you have been saved through faith’ (tē chariti este sesōsmenoi dia pisteōs) is one of the most celebrated formulations in Pauline theology. Grace is the ultimate cause of salvation, faith the instrumental means, and both are ‘the gift of God’ (v. 8)—not produced by human effort.

The noun poiēma (v. 10) denotes something made or crafted. Believers are God’s workmanship—his handiwork, his masterpiece. The passive implication is clear: believers do not create themselves; they are created by God ‘in Christ Jesus to do good works, which God prepared in advance for us to do.’ Even the good works that follow salvation are prepared by God.

refs.note: Paul’s teaching on union with Christ in his death and resurrection parallels Eph 2:5–6. In both passages, believers participate in Christ’s death-resurrection-exaltation, though Ephesians uniquely adds that believers are already ‘seated with Christ in the heavenly realms.’
Paul’s condensed soteriology in Titus closely parallels Eph 2:4–9: salvation is not by works of righteousness but by God’s mercy, through the renewal of the Holy Spirit, justified by grace.

notes.note: The human condition is not merely sick but dead—spiritually dead in transgressions and sins. MacArthur emphasises the total depravity implied: dead people cannot contribute to their own resurrection. The three masters of the unregenerate—the world, the devil, and the flesh—are comprehensive: external pressure, supernatural deception, and internal corruption.

‘But because of his great love for us, God...’—the adversative ‘but’ (de) is the most important conjunction in the Bible, marking the transition from death to life. MacArthur notes the already-realised eschatology: believers are not merely promised future resurrection but are already made alive, raised, and seated with Christ in the heavenly realms.

The salvation formula is definitive: by grace, through faith, not by works, the gift of God. MacArthur insists that ‘this’ (touto) in ‘this is not from yourselves’ refers to the entire salvation package (grace + faith), not merely to grace or faith in isolation. Verse 10 prevents antinomianism: believers are created for good works, though works are the result, not the cause, of salvation.

Calvin reads the condition of spiritual death as proof of total inability: the dead cannot raise themselves. The three masters (world, devil, flesh) cooperate to keep humanity in bondage. Calvin insists that the phrase ‘by nature objects of wrath’ (v. 3) refers to original sin: the corruption is not merely habitual but constitutional.

Calvin’s exposition of these verses became foundational for Reformed soteriology. Grace is the efficient cause of salvation; faith is the instrumental means; God’s purpose is the final cause. Calvin emphasises that even faith is ‘the gift of God’—not a human work but a divinely granted capacity to receive Christ. The ‘good works prepared in advance’ (v. 10) demonstrate that sanctification is as much God’s work as justification.

The parenthetical statement chariti este sesōsmenoi (by grace you have been saved) uses the periphrastic perfect: the salvation is a completed action with ongoing results. The NET translators note this is repeated in v. 8, forming an inclusio around the grace-salvation theme.

The demonstrative pronoun touto (this) is neuter, while charis (grace) and pistis (faith) are both feminine, suggesting touto refers to the entire preceding concept—the salvation described in vv. 4–8—rather than to grace or faith specifically.

Lincoln identifies the passage as a once-and-now contrast: the readers’ former existence in death (vv. 1–3) and their present existence in grace (vv. 4–10). Lincoln notes the cosmological dimension: the ‘ruler of the kingdom of the air’ (v. 2) reflects the ancient belief in a sublunar realm inhabited by malevolent powers. The exaltation language of vv. 5–6 parallels 1:20: what God did for Christ (raised, seated) he has also done for believers in Christ.

O’Brien reads the passage as a tightly constructed theological argument. The triad of death—world, devil, flesh—is not merely illustrative but comprehensive: it encompasses the environmental, supernatural, and internal dimensions of human bondage. O’Brien emphasises that the ‘but God’ (ho de theos, v. 4) is the turning point: God’s initiative, motivated by love and mercy, is the sole explanation for the transition from death to life. The goal of the transition is doxological: ‘in the coming ages’ God will display the riches of his grace (v. 7)—believers are trophies of grace on eternal display.

hist.context: Ephesians 2:1–10 is one of the most systematic descriptions of salvation in the NT. Paul moves through three stages: the human condition before Christ (vv. 1–3)—dead in transgressions, following the world, the devil, and the flesh; the divine intervention (vv. 4–7)—God, rich in mercy, made us alive, raised us up, and seated us with Christ in the heavenly realms; and the theological explanation (vv. 8–10)—salvation is by grace through faith, not by works, so that no one can boast. The past-present-future structure of vv. 5–7 is significant: God has already made believers alive (past), seated them in the heavenly realms (present positional reality), ‘in order that in the coming ages he might show the incomparable riches of his grace’ (future).

heb.paragraph: The declaration ‘he himself is our peace’ (autos gar estin hē eirēnē hēmōn, v. 14) identifies Christ not merely as the agent of peace but as the embodiment of peace. The Hebrew background (shalom) adds depth: peace as comprehensive wholeness, the restoration of right relationships—between God and humanity, and between Jew and Gentile.

The noun mesotoichon (v. 14) appears only here in the NT. The ‘dividing wall of hostility’ has been variously identified with the literal barrier in the Jerusalem temple separating the Court of the Gentiles from the inner courts (trespass by Gentiles was punishable by death), or with the Torah’s regulations that created social separation between Jews and Gentiles.

refs.note: Paul’s parallel statement of reconciliation in Colossians uses similar ‘once alienated, now reconciled’ language, though Ephesians uniquely emphasises the Jewish-Gentile dimension.
Isaiah’s prophecy—‘Peace, peace, to those far and near’—is echoed in Eph 2:17, where Christ ‘came and preached peace to you who were far away and peace to those who were near.’
Peter’s temple imagery—believers as ‘living stones’ being built into a ‘spiritual house’ with Christ as the cornerstone—provides the closest NT parallel to Eph 2:19–22.

notes.note: Paul catalogues five disabilities of the Gentiles before Christ: separate from Christ, excluded from citizenship in Israel, foreigners to the covenants, without hope, without God. MacArthur notes this is a description of complete spiritual destitution—no Messiah, no covenant, no promises, no hope, no God.

Christ’s blood has bridged the distance. The ‘dividing wall of hostility’ is destroyed—Paul may be alluding to the literal temple barrier or, more broadly, to the Torah’s ceremonial regulations that created social separation. Christ created ‘one new humanity’ out of the two: this is not the absorption of Gentiles into Israel or the elimination of Israel, but something genuinely new.

Christ preached peace to both far (Gentiles) and near (Jews). Through him both have access to the Father by one Spirit (v. 18)—another trinitarian formulation. The temple metaphor (vv. 19–22) presents the church as a new temple in which God dwells by his Spirit, replacing the Jerusalem temple as the locus of divine presence.

Calvin reads the ‘dividing wall’ as the ceremonial law that separated Jew from Gentile. Christ abolished this wall not by annulling God’s moral standards but by fulfilling the law’s requirements and removing the ceremonial regulations that created the barrier. The ‘one new humanity’ is the church, in which ethnic distinctions no longer determine spiritual status.

Calvin emphasises that the church is a temple built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets—not as its cornerstone (that is Christ alone) but as the authoritative witnesses whose teaching constitutes the doctrinal foundation. The church grows as a living structure, with each believer fitted into the whole by the Spirit’s work.

The articular phrase to mesotoichon tou phragmou (the dividing wall of the fence/barrier) may refer to the physical barrier in the temple or to the Torah as a metaphorical wall. The NET translators favour the metaphorical reading, with the law’s commandments and regulations as the ‘fence’ that separated Jew and Gentile.

The participle katargēsas (having abolished) applies to ‘the law with its commands and regulations’ (ton nomon tōn entolōn en dogmasin). The NET notes that this does not mean the moral law is annulled but that the Torah’s function as a barrier between Jew and Gentile has been removed.

Lincoln identifies this passage as the ecclesiological centre of Ephesians. The ‘once-now’ schema shifts from individual soteriology (vv. 1–10) to corporate reconciliation (vv. 11–22). Lincoln notes that the ‘one new humanity’ (heis kainos anthrōpos, v. 15) is a genuinely novel entity—neither Jew nor Gentile but something that transcends and encompasses both. The temple metaphor (vv. 19–22) replaces the Jerusalem temple: God’s dwelling is no longer a building but a community of believers from all nations.

O’Brien reads the passage in two panels: the problem (vv. 11–12, alienation) and the solution (vv. 13–22, reconciliation). The key christological affirmation—‘he himself is our peace’ (v. 14)—is both relational (peace between Jew and Gentile) and redemptive (peace between humanity and God). O’Brien notes the vertical and horizontal dimensions: Christ reconciled both groups ‘to God through the cross’ (v. 16), simultaneously destroying the hostility between them. The architectural metaphors of vv. 19–22 progress from political (‘fellow citizens’) to domestic (‘household’) to sacred (‘temple’), each image conveying greater intimacy.

hist.context: The second half of Ephesians 2 addresses the corporate dimension of salvation: the reconciliation of Jew and Gentile into one new humanity. The ‘once-now’ contrast continues from vv. 1–10 but shifts to ethnic/covenantal categories. The Gentiles were formerly ‘separate from Christ, excluded from citizenship in Israel, foreigners to the covenants of the promise, without hope and without God’ (v. 12). But now in Christ, they who were far away have been brought near ‘by the blood of Christ’ (v. 13). Christ’s death destroyed the barrier between Jew and Gentile, abolishing the law with its regulations, and created ‘one new humanity’ (hena kainon anthrōpon, v. 15) out of the two, making peace. The passage climaxes with three architectural metaphors: believers are ‘fellow citizens’ (v. 19), ‘members of his household’ (v. 19), and a ‘holy temple in the Lord’ (v. 21) built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, with Christ as the cornerstone.

rows.label: vv.1–3
rows.text: Dead in transgressions: the world, the devil, the flesh

rows.label: vv.4–7
rows.text: But God: made alive, raised up, seated with Christ

rows.label: vv.8–10
rows.text: By grace through faith: the gift of God, God’s workmanship

rows.label: vv.11–12
rows.text: Once: Gentiles excluded, without hope, without God

rows.label: vv.13–16
rows.text: Now: brought near by blood; one new humanity; wall destroyed

rows.label: vv.17–18
rows.text: Peace preached to far and near; access to the Father

rows.label: vv.19–22
rows.text: Fellow citizens, household, holy temple; Christ the cornerstone

lit.note: Ephesians 2 moves from individual salvation (vv. 1–10) to corporate reconciliation (vv. 11–22). The first half establishes salvation by grace through faith as God’s definitive work. The second half reveals the social and ecclesiological implications: Christ has destroyed the wall between Jew and Gentile, creating one new humanity and building a living temple.

themes.note: Mercy and Faith share the highest scores as the chapter proclaims salvation by grace through faith for dead sinners. Sovereignty and Covenant are prominent through God’s initiative in redemption and the incorporation of Gentiles into the covenant community. Holiness appears through the temple imagery.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

thread.text: Peter’s temple imagery—believers as ‘living stones’ being built into a ‘spiritual house’ with Christ as the cornerstone—provides the closest NT parallel to Eph 2:19–22.

discourse.thesis: The same power that raised Christ from the dead has raised dead sinners to life in him — creating one new humanity from Jew and Gentile, reconciled to God through the cross.

nodes.text: The condition before grace: dead in trespasses and sins (νεκροὺς τοῖς παραπτώμασιν), walking according to the age of this world, according to the prince of the power of the air, among the sons of disobedience. 'We all' — Paul includes himself — lived in the passions of the flesh, carrying out the desires of body and mind, children of wrath by nature.

The hinge of the letter — two words that reverse everything. God, being rich in mercy (πλούσιος ὢν ἐν ἐλέει), because of the great love with which he loved us, made us alive together with Christ (συνεζωοποίησεν), raised us up with him (συνήγειρεν), and seated us with him in the heavenly places (συνεκάθισεν). The three 'syn-' (with-) compound verbs are unique in the NT — Paul is constructing a participatory theology of union.

children.text: The purpose: so that in the coming ages God might show the immeasurable riches of his grace in kindness toward us in Christ Jesus. The present experience of grace is an eternal exhibition of divine generosity.

nodes.text: The theological summary: saved by grace through faith — and this is not your own doing, it is the gift of God, not a result of works so that no one may boast. We are his workmanship (ποίημα — poem/work of art), created in Christ Jesus for good works that God prepared beforehand.

children.text: The dialectic: not by works (so no boasting) → for good works (that we might walk in them). Salvation is not by works but it is most certainly for works. Grace does not eliminate ethical demand — it enables it from the inside.

nodes.text: Gentile readers are called to remember their pre-Christ condition: separated from Christ, alienated from the commonwealth of Israel, strangers to the covenants of promise, having no hope and without God in the world. But now — the second great reversal — in Christ Jesus those who were far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ.

children.text: The five-fold deficit: no Christ, no citizenship in Israel, no covenants, no hope, no God. This is not poetic exaggeration but theological diagnosis — the Gentile condition outside the covenant was genuinely hopeless.

nodes.marker: For he himself is our peace (Αὐτὸς γάρ ἐστιν ἡ εἰρήνη ἡμῶν)
nodes.text: Christ is our peace who made both groups one and broke down the dividing wall of hostility (τὸ μεσότοιχον τοῦ φραγμοῦ). He abolished in his flesh the law of commandments expressed in ordinances, to create in himself one new man in place of the two. Both have access through him in one Spirit to the Father.

children.text: Two reconciliation purposes: (1) create one new humanity (εἷς καινὸς ἄνθρωπος) from the two; (2) reconcile both to God in one body through the cross, killing the hostility. The cross is simultaneously vertical (God-human) and horizontal (Jew-Gentile) reconciliation.

nodes.text: The new identity of Gentile believers: no longer strangers and aliens but fellow citizens with the saints and members of the household of God, built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Christ Jesus being the cornerstone. The whole structure grows into a holy temple — the church as God's new dwelling place in the Spirit.

children.text: The temple metaphor develops: Christ the cornerstone (ἀκρογωνιαῖον) determines the alignment of the whole building. Each believer is a stone; the corporate structure is a dwelling place (κατοικητήριον) of God — not just a gathering of individuals but an architectural unity.

discourse.note: Ephesians 2 contains two of Paul's greatest rhetorical turns: 'But God' (v. 4) and 'But now' (v. 13). The chapter moves from individual salvation history (vv. 1-10) to corporate ecclesiology (vv. 11-22) — both are essential, and neither is complete without the other. The 'dividing wall' (v. 14) likely alludes to the temple barrier separating the court of the Gentiles, which Christ's death has permanently removed.

coaching.tip: 'By grace you have been saved through faith—and this is not from yourselves, it is the gift of God' (v. 8). The grammar is emphatic: salvation, faith, the entire package—gift. 'Not by works, so that no one can boast' (v. 9). Yet verse 10 adds: 'created in Christ Jesus to do good works.' Saved by grace, saved for works. The order matters.

'He himself is our peace' (v. 14). Peace isn't a policy Jesus implements—it's his person. The 'dividing wall of hostility' (v. 14) evokes the Temple barrier separating Gentile courts from Jewish worship. Christ demolished it, creating 'one new humanity' (v. 15). Reconciliation with God produces reconciliation with others.
